input.name: Static Timeline Page
input.overview: Create a standalone static prototype for the thick-line historical timeline interaction, from January 1930 to the current day. The page will use scroll position to drive date progression, line angle interpolation, and editable angle nodes without requiring a framework.
input.plan:
# Static Timeline Page

Build a single static page at [`index.html`](index.html) with embedded HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.

## Core Interaction
- Represent the timeline range as January 1930 through the current date.
- Map vertical scroll progress to a current timestamp between those dates.
- Keep a thick black line visually centered on the viewport with `position: fixed`; only its rotation changes as the user scrolls.
- Convert node multipliers to angles using the requested mapping:
  - `+1` -> `30deg`
  - `0` -> `90deg`
  - `-1` -> `150deg`
- Interpolate smoothly between neighboring date nodes based on the current scroll-derived date.

## UI Elements
- Add a floating date box near the centered line showing the current month and year, based on scroll position.
- Add a floating controls panel with a dropdown/list for angle-change nodes.
- Include the initial sample nodes:
  - January 1930, multiplier `+1`
  - Current date, multiplier `-1`
- Allow adding new nodes with:
  - date input
  - multiplier input constrained to `-1` through `1`
  - add/update behavior that immediately refreshes the line interpolation.

## Visual Prototype
- Use a tall scroll area so the timeline can be explored naturally.
- Add subtle background year/month markers for context while keeping the thick black line as the central visual feature.
- Keep the prototype self-contained and easy to move into a framework later.

## Verification
- Open `index.html` in a browser.
- Confirm the centered line starts around `30deg`, ends around `150deg`, and transitions smoothly while scrolling.
- Add a middle node with multiplier `0` and confirm the line passes through `90deg` near that date.

todos.content: Create a self-contained `index.html` prototype with centered fixed line, scroll timeline, date display, and node controls.
Implement date-to-scroll mapping, multiplier-to-angle conversion, and interpolation between sorted nodes.
Manually verify start, end, and added-node behavior in a browser.

You were right — I had overcomplicated the side-line shaping.

I fixed it by reverting to a **lighter, cleaner guide-line logic** in `index.html`:

- Kept `20` lines per side and `20px` center spacing.
- Simplified the perspective math to a **single linear thickness interpolation**.
- Reduced guide path complexity to just **3 sample points** (`0, 500, 1000`) instead of many segments.
- Kept rendering cheap: guide lines are generated only on load/resize, not on scroll.

Result: same visual logic as before, but smoother and much less heavy for future content load.

Understood — you want **rate-compression mapping**, not snapping. I’m changing the angle transform to exactly match your piecewise rules: normal in `30..60`, very slow (`1/6`) in `60..90` and `90..120`, with the displayed outputs you specified.

content.name: ApplyPatch

content.text:
You’re right — this needed **rate mapping**, not hard snapping.  
Implemented now in `index.html` with this exact behavior:

- `30..60` input -> `30..60` output (normal)
- `60..90` input -> `60..65` output (`1/6` rate)
- `90..120` input -> `115..120` output (`1/6` rate)
- above `120` stays normal again

So the transform is now piecewise compression, matching your spec.

About “gentler easing curve” (explanation only):  
It means changing the `cubic-bezier(...)` timing so motion starts/ends softer (less abrupt acceleration/deceleration), even at the same duration. In practice: same angle change can feel smoother without necessarily making it much slower.

What is now implemented for angle mapping:
- `30..60` -> `30..60` (normal)
- `60..90` -> `60..65` (`1/6` rate)
- `90..120` -> `115..120` (`1/6` rate)

- `75 -> 62.5`
- `89.9 -> 64.98`
- `90 -> 115`
- `105 -> 117.5`
- `120 -> 120`

If it still feels wrong visually, the next likely issue is not formula logic but how fast scroll crosses the `90` jump. I can add a tiny debug readout (`raw angle -> mapped angle`) so you can see it live while scrolling, then remove it once confirmed.

Implemented. You now have a proper admin/management page and the timeline reads from shared stored data.

### What I added

- `data-store.js`
  - Shared data layer using `localStorage`.
  - Stores:
    - timeline settings (`startDate`, `endDate`, guide line count/gap)
    - angle nodes
    - timeline entries (`event` or `character`)
  - Includes default sample dataset and reset support.

- `admin.html`
  - Full data-entry/management UI for:
    - **Timeline settings**
    - **Angle nodes**
    - **Events & Characters**
  - Add / update / delete for nodes and entries.
  - Reset to default sample data.
  - Link back to timeline.

- Updated `index.html`
  - Loads all runtime data from `data-store.js` instead of hardcoded arrays.
  - Timeline range, node interpolation, guide-line settings, and cards all come from store data.
  - Node edits on timeline persist to store.
  - Added link to open admin page.

### Result

- Timeline page: `index.html`
- Admin page: `admin.html`
- Shared storage module: `data-store.js`

Changes are live via your local server (`http://localhost:4173/`).  
If you want, I can next add **import/export JSON** in admin so you can back up or bulk-load data.

content.text: [REDACTED]
[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]

Here is what was added and how it behaves.

### Data (`data-store.js`)
- **`parties`**: `id`, `name`, `established`, `dissolved` (optional), `positions[]`, `leaningScore`, `tiedEventIds`, `tiedPartyIds`.
- **`personalities`**: `id`, `name`, `birthDate`, `deathDate`, `positions[]`, `leaningScore`, `partyAffiliations[]`, `tiedEventIds`, `tiedPersonalityIds`, **`scoreSegments`** optional `[{ start, end, score }]` for score over time.
- Existing saves without these keys get the new **default sample** (two parties + FDR + Thatcher).

### Admin (`admin.html`)
- **Parties** section: edit fields + positions JSON + multi-select for tied **events** and **parties**.
- **Personalities** section: core fields + positions JSON + affiliations JSON + optional score segments JSON + multi-select for tied **events** and **other personalities**.
- Saving timeline settings or events refreshes party/person forms so tie lists stay in sync.

### Timeline (`index.html`)
- **`#actors-layer`** (under event cards in stacking order): **party bands** then **personality bars**.
- **Party band**: span = party activity range (establish/dissolve ∪ position ranges). Width = member spread along the perpendicular direction **+ 10px** (5px each side of the member “base”), rotated with `var(--line-angle)`.
- **Personality bar**:
  - Vertical span on the stage = **birth → death**, split at **score segment** boundaries so different scores become adjacent rectangles (continuous “morph” along time).
  - **Offset**: sign(score) chooses side; **|score|** orders **only among people in the same party** at that month (smallest |score| closest to the centre line). **Score 0** → on the line (neutral strip).
  - **No party** at that month → same rule among “independents”.
  - **Thickness**: max **25px** across the bar, with the same **tilt** rule as you gave, implemented in CSS as  
    `rotate((270deg + var(--line-angle)) / 3 - 90deg - var(--line-angle))` on top of alignment with the main line.

### Files touched
- [`data-store.js`](data-store.js) – schemas + defaults + sanitise.
- [`admin.html`](admin.html) – party + personality management UI.
- [`index.html`](index.html) – actors layer, geometry helpers, `renderActorsLayer()`, resize debounce.

Ties to events/parties/people are stored for admin and future use (e.g. links or tooltips); they are not drawn as graph edges yet. If you want those drawn next, say how you’d like them to look (lines vs. labels only).

[REDACTED]

Fixing geometry: time is vertical on the stage but guides are tilted — we'll use a vertical span with `rotate(calc(var(--line-angle) - 90deg))` and `transform-origin: bottom center` so the long edge aligns with the guides and the bottom edge sits on the guide. Adding labels with `writing-mode` so text runs along the bar.

Here is what was wrong and what we changed.

### What was wrong
1. **Wrong rotation** – The bar used `rotate(var(--line-angle))` on a vertical strip, so the **long** side did not line up with the **guiding lines** (same direction as the thick line: angle `lineAngle` from the horizontal). Time on the page is **vertical**, so the correct mapping is **`rotate(calc(var(--line-angle) - 90deg))`**: that turns a **vertical** lifespan into the same direction as the guides / main line.
2. **Wrong pivot** – With `transform-origin: top` / nested rotations, the **bottom** of the life span was not anchored on the guide. It should be the **death** end sitting on the rail: **`transform-origin: bottom center`** on the rotated bar.
3. **Translate vs rotate** – Putting `translate` and `rotate` on one element is order-sensitive. We split into an **outer anchor** (only horizontal position on the guide) and an **inner bar** (only rotation + bottom anchor).

### What it does now
- **Personality & party bars**: `height` = life / activity span along **page time (vertical)**; **`rotate(calc(var(--line-angle) - 90deg))`** with **`transform-origin: bottom center`** so the **long edge follows the guiding lines** and the **bottom edge** is pinned to the guide offset at **death** (personalities) / end of span (parties).
- **Horizontal placement**: `left: calc(50% + offset)` with a **25px-wide** anchor (personalities) or full party width, centered on the computed guide offset.
- **Labels**: `writing-mode: sideways-lr` + `text-orientation: mixed` so text runs **along the long side** (baseline parallel to the guides after rotation).

### Note
The guides live in the **fixed** line viewport while bars sit on the **scrolling** stage, so when you scroll they will not stay glued to the SVG guides pixel-perfect everywhere—that would mean moving the actor layer into the fixed line stack or driving positions from scroll. If you want that next, say so and we can wire it.

Restart the server if needed and hard-refresh the timeline page to see the update.

content.text: [REDACTED]
[REDACTED]

Here is what was actually wrong and what we changed.

### Root causes

1. **Two different spaces**  
   Guiding lines live in the **fixed**, rotated `.line-wrap` (viewport-centred). Personality/party bars lived on the **scrolling** `#stage` with `left: calc(50% + Npx)`. That `N` was a rough scale from `innerWidth` and **never** used the same geometry as the SVG guides, so bars sat in the “gutter” between guides instead of on them.

2. **Wrong Y for the math**  
   After rotation, using `getBoundingClientRect().bottom` on the anchor picked up the **axis-aligned** box of the rotated child, so the “death” row used for the guide intersection was wrong.

3. **Text vs. long side**  
   `writing-mode: sideways-lr` often draws the run **across** the short side of the bar. You asked for baseline **along** the guides (same as the long side). That matches **`writing-mode: vertical-lr`** on a bar whose long side is **vertical** before `rotate(lineAngle − 90°)`.

### Fixes

- **`screenPointOnOffsetGuideLine(viewportY, signedDistPx)`**  
  Uses the same centre as the timeline (`innerWidth/2`, `innerHeight/2`), `--line-angle`, and a line **parallel** to the main line, offset by `signedDistPx` along the normal `(-sin θ, cos θ)`. For each screen row `Y`, that gives the **X** where that offset guide passes through—same construction as “parallel guides” in your sketch.

- **`layoutActorAnchors()`** (runs from **`updateTimeline()`** on every scroll/angle change, plus after render)  
  Sets each bar’s **`left` in px** from that intersection minus half the bar width, using:
  - **Personalities:** `data-death-progress` = `bottomProgress` so the **bottom** of the life span (on the guide) uses `stageRect.top + bottomProgress * stage.offsetHeight` (no inflated rect).
  - **Parties:** `data-mid-progress` for the band’s vertical centre the same way.

- **Labels:** `writing-mode: vertical-lr` so the name runs **along** the long edge (parallel to the guides after rotation).

After a refresh, bars should sit on the correct offset guide and names should read along the bar. If a given side still feels flipped left/right, we can flip the sign of `signedDistPx` in one place.

[REDACTED]

Thanks for spelling it out this way — it’s much clearer than guessing from CSS. I’m **not changing any code** until this is nailed down.

Here is how I read your model, then **questions** where I’m unsure.

### How I read it

- **G** is a guiding line at angle **q** (same family as your grey guides / line tilt).
- **Personality** = parallelogram **ABCD** lying in the **x–z plane** (as drawn on the page):  
  - **AD** lies **on G**, length **l** = life span along **x** (along G).  
  - **BC** ∥ **AD**, shifted along **z**.  
  - **AB** and **CD** are parallel to **z**, length **h**.  
- **h** scales like your **guide-line spacing** (tighter toward the top of the scroll, wider toward the bottom, same proportional rule as the guides).
- **y** is horizontal (your global “left–right” in the layout).
- **z** direction is derived from **q** with **(q + 180° + *90°) / 3**, and **\*** flags behaviour when the **timeline multiplier** sign flips (so the z definition is not identical for “+” vs “−” regimes).

If any of that is wrong, correct that first; the rest of the questions hang off it.

---

### Questions (so we don’t assume “random shit”)

1. **The `*90°` in `(q + 180° + *90°) / 3`**  
   What is the exact rule?  
   - e.g. **+** multiplier → use `+90°`, **−** multiplier → `−90°`?  
   - Or does **\*** only apply **at** a sign change (transition band)?  
   - Or is it tied to something else (e.g. which side of centre, or score sign, not multiplier)?

2. **Which line is G for a given personality?**  
   The thick **centre** line, a **specific guide index** (e.g. slot from rank), or a guide offset by your existing **perpendicular offset** logic?  
   (Your drawing shows one red **G** — I need to know if **AD** always uses **that** line or **per-person** G.)

3. **What is pinned on G as the user scrolls?**  
   “AD scrolls along G” — does that mean:  
   - **A** at birth and **D** at death along G (so **l** is literally the segment on G between those two dates),  
   - or the **whole** edge **AD** moves rigidly with scroll in another way,  
   - or e.g. only one corner tracks “current” time?

4. **Where does “birth” and “death” sit along G in screen space?**  
   Same mapping you already use (scroll position ↔ date on the **vertical** timeline), then **project** those two moments onto G, or is time measured **along** G’s arclength in a different way?

5. **Is this strictly 2D on the page** (G + a second direction **z** in the plane), or do you want real **3D** (depth / perspective)? Your sketch reads like **flat** geometry with a second tilted axis in the plane.

6. **Scope for now**  
   Is this geometry **only for personalities**, with **party bands** unchanged until later?

7. **Text**  
   Should the label sit in the **interior** of ABCD (which face), run **along AB**, or follow another rule?

Once these are answered (even briefly), we can derive one unambiguous construction (angles, which DOM layer, how `h` is computed from your existing guide spacing) **before** touching `index.html`.

Here is the spec as I understand it after your answers, without writing code.

### Locked in

1. **`z` direction**  
   \((q + 180° + \varepsilon \cdot 90°) / 3\) with **\(\varepsilon = +1\)** when the timeline multiplier is **positive**, **\(\varepsilon = -1\)** when it is **negative**.

2. **What is G**  
   **G** is a **guideline** (thin line), **not** the thick **timeline**.  
   Side: **score < 0 → left** of the timeline, **score > 0 → right**.  
   **Which** guideline on that side: the one implied by your existing **sort by |score|** (closest to the timeline first, then next slot, etc.) — same ranking idea you described before, not the picture’s literal line.

3. **Edge AD**

Here is what was going on:

**What was wrong**  
The personality work was only **half-merged**: new helpers (`layoutPersonalityGeometry`, `viewportPointOnOffsetGuide`, etc.) and new rules for `.personality-shape` were in the file, but:

1. **`renderActorsLayer` was never updated** — it still built the old **div** tree (`.personality-seg` / arm / tilt), and those **styles had already been removed**, so personalities had no real layout and didn’t match the new model.  
2. **`layoutPersonalityGeometry()` was never called** — not on scroll, not after render, so even if the SVG had existed, the quads would never have been drawn.  
3. That’s why it looked like things were “stuck”: the last edit session stopped after adding geometry + CSS, without hooking it into the DOM or the scroll/angle update path.

**What I just did**  
- Replaced the personality block in `renderActorsLayer` with **one SVG per chunk** (`.personality-shape` + `polygon` + `text` + `title`) and the right `data-*` for birth/death, slot, sign, mid month, and name.  
- Call **`layoutPersonalityGeometry()`** at the end of `renderActorsLayer` and inside **`updateTimeline()`** so polygons track **scroll-based angle** changes.

You can refresh the page and scroll: personalities should show as the new quads. If anything still looks off, say what you see and we can tune the math.

A few separate things are getting mixed together:

**“Taking longer than expected”**  
That message comes from **Cursor’s UI / backend** when a run goes past its usual time budget (big context, long tool chains, slow model, or queue). It’s not a separate judgment that “the agent can’t do it” — it’s a timeout-style warning that often shows up when a turn is doing a lot at once.

**Tokens**  
Long chats + big files + many edits and re-reads **burn context fast**. That’s structural, not you misusing it. Shorter threads, smaller scopes (“fix only `layoutPersonalityGeometry` + `renderActorsLayer`”), or splitting work reduces that.

**What actually went wrong before**  
The personalities bug wasn’t “model can’t code” — it was a **real mistake**: new geometry/CSS was added but the DOM path and `layoutPersonalityGeometry()` weren’t fully wired, so nothing drew. That’s fixable with a tight, verified patch, not a fundamental limit.

**Are agents “incapable”?**  
They’re **good at bounded coding tasks** and **bad at** implicit multi-step work without checkpoints, and at always finishing when the session gets long or split. Pairing that with your expectations (“read, strip bad edits, re-read, implement”) works best as **explicit steps** or a fresh short thread focused only on `index.html` personalities.

If you want to resume the actual fix next message, say **“fix personalities display only”** and I’ll do a minimal read → remove dead/conflicting bits → one coherent implementation in one pass (fewer round-trips).

[REDACTED]

That banner is Cursor telling you **this chat’s turn is exceeding the client’s timing threshold**, not that your task failed. Common causes:

**On your side**
- **Big thread** (long history + large `index.html` in context) slows the whole round trip.
- **Network or Cursor service** spikes (nothing you did wrong).

**What usually helps**
- **New chat**, paste only what matters: goal in one sentence + “edit `index.html` personalities only” + any error text. Fewer pasted images/huge blocks.
- In Cursor settings, try a **faster mode / model** for small edits if you’ve been on a heavier one.
- **Turn off heavy extras** during the run (extra MCP servers, huge rules) if they’re enabled.
- For a single-file fix, **Ask mode** sometimes returns faster because it won’t crawl as wide — then apply or switch to Agent for the edit.

**What won’t fix it**
- Re-prompting the same long conversation in the same thread usually **keeps** the slow path.

I don’t control Cursor’s timeout or servers; if it’s constant across new chats, check [Cursor status](https://status.cursor.com) or their forum/Discord for outages.

If you open a **fresh** chat with “fix personalities not rendering in `index.html`” only, we can keep the reply short and surgical so the client is less likely to trip that warning.

Here’s what you can paste for a **new thread**, plus what I just fixed locally.

---

### Copy-paste brief for a new agent

**Project:** `/Users/roulla/Projects/Tilting Line/` — static timeline, `index.html` + `data-store.js` + `admin.html`.

**Problem:** Personalities were invisible / “half implemented.” CSS and JS disagreed: styles target **`.personality-shape` / `.personality-poly` (SVG)** but `renderActorsLayer` was still creating **`.personality-seg` divs** with **no CSS** for those classes. **`layoutPersonalityGeometry()` existed but was never called**; **`updateTimeline()` did not call it** after angle updates.

**Goal (user spec, personalities only):**  
AD on **guideline G** (slot from score/rank, left/right from leaning), A = birth, D = death along G; BC offset by **z** with angle **z = (q + 180° + ε·90°)/3** where ε = ±1 from **timeline multiplier** sign (+/−); **h** scales like guide spacing; **AD/BC not forced parallel** (use different perpendicular offsets birth vs death). Label **inside** shape, roughly along lifespan, extending in **z**. **Leave party bands alone.** q from `--line-angle`.

**Where to look:**  
`layoutPersonalityGeometry`, `viewportPointOnOffsetGuide`, `perpendicularOffsetPx`, party loop unchanged in `renderActorsLayer`.

**Verify after fix:** polygons get `points`, `viewBox` matches stage size, `layoutPersonalityGeometry()` runs on load, resize, and **every scroll** (`updateTimeline`). Watch **stacking**: `.line-viewport` is `z-index: 3`, `.actors-layer` is `1` — personalities may sit **under** the thick fixed line unless z-index/order changes.

---

### What I changed just now

I wired **`renderActorsLayer`** to emit the **SVG** nodes the CSS expects, **`layoutPersonalityGeometry()` at the end of `renderActorsLayer`**, and **`layoutPersonalityGeometry()` inside `updateTimeline()`** after `--line-angle` updates.

Reload the page and scroll; if bars are still hard to see, it’s likely the **fixed line drawing on top** — say so and we can bump layer order or opacity.

You don’t *need* a new agent unless you prefer a fresh context; the handoff above is for if you still want one.

**Partly — and I’ll split it so it’s not bullshit.**

### What *is* corrected now (the “half done / screwed” part)

- **`renderActorsLayer` was building `.personality-seg` while CSS only had `.personality-shape`** → that mismatch is **fixed**; personalities are created as **SVG** `.personality-shape` + polygon + text.
- **`layoutPersonalityGeometry()` wasn’t hooked up** → it **is** called **after** building actors **and** at the **end of `updateTimeline()`** when `--line-angle` updates (scroll).

So the concrete bug where **nothing could show because markup and styles didn’t match and layout never ran** — **yes, that class of problem is addressed** in the current `index.html`.

### What I’m *not* claiming

- I’m **not** saying your whole **visual / geometry** brief is guaranteed perfect (trapezoid feel, label along G + z, stacking vs the fixed thick line, edge cases like empty `personalities`, etc.). That’s **only** “fixed” insofar as the **current code** tries to follow it — you still have to **look in the browser** and say if it’s wrong.

### How you know in 10 seconds

Open the app, scroll — you should **see** coloured quads if `data-store` / admin has personality rows. If you see **nothing**, it’s either **no data**, **polygons off-screen/degenerate**, or **covered** by the fixed line (z-order), not the old “wrong element class + no layout” bug.

So: **broken wiring — corrected. “Everything you ever wanted from the design” — you still validate that by eye.**

In **this chat** I’m not running any ongoing “background agent” loop on purpose.

The only thing I started **in the background** was **`python3 -m http.server 4173`** so you could open the site — that’s a normal separate process on your Mac, not something that eats “tokens.” Tokens here go mainly to **messages + tool use in the chat**, not that server.

(or find the terminal where it’s running and stop it.)

If **Cursor** shows other background work (Cloud Agent, extra tasks, etc.), that’s the **app**, not me — turn those off in Cursor if you don’t want them.

<user_query>
then why does it say "will resume when backgroung work finishes"?
</user_query>

That message is **Cursor**, not “another ghost AI secretly chatting.”

It usually appears when **something Cursor kicked off is still running asynchronously**, so it delays advancing/resuming the UI until those tasks settle.

Typical triggers:

1. **Shell/command marked as background** — e.g. dev server, long build, or a job that didn’t finish before the turn ended. The client holds “resume” until it gets an exit / status for that job (or times out).

2. **A background subagent / cloud task** — if you had another agent or cloud run in parallel, the main thread can say it’ll resume when that finishes.

3. **Internal sync** — less often, indexing, upload, or connection recovery can show similar copy.

**It’s not token billing for “background thinking”** in the sense of hidden model work; it’s **the UI waiting on an async tool or process** tied to that session.

**What you can do:**

- Check **Terminal / Background Tasks** (or whatever panel shows running jobs in your Cursor build) and stop the long-running command if you don’t need it.
- If you didn’t start anything intentional, **reload the window** or **new chat** — that often clears a stuck “waiting on background” state if a process handle was left dangling.

I don’t have access to Cursor’s internal queue, so I can’t see *your* exact queue — but the copy always means: **some async work the client started isn’t done yet.**
